_Washington_ \| President Donald Trump has threatened Iran with a wave of new attacks if it does not open the blockaded Strait of Hormuz, in an expletive-laden social media rant that critics have seized on as evidence of his crude and unchecked use of power.

Hours after his victorious announcement that the US military had saved [two stranded US airmen](https://www.afr.com/link/follow-20180101-p5zlf9), who had ejected into Iran after facing enemy fire, Trump said further strikes would come on Tuesday if Tehran did not comply.

BlueScope Steel has repeatedly told shareholders that its US business is worth substantially more than its spurned suitors put on the table. Now it’s ready to prove it.

The $11.4 billion company is preparing an information memorandum to kickstart an auction for the North American steelmaking business, which brings in more than half of the group’s earnings, sources told Street Talk on the condition of anonymity. It is taking advice from UBS, Herbert Smith Freehills Kramer and [Tanarra Capital’s John Wylie.](https://www.afr.com/street-talk/wylie-moonlighting-at-bluescope-20260312-p5o9tq)

Zeus Street Greek, a Mediterranean food chain with 45 locations across the country, is set to roll out more quick-service restaurants serving lamb pitas and Spartan bowls after taking on external funding.

Mid-market private equity firm Whiteoak has taken a significant stake in the $100 million-plus revenue business, marking the third investment from its Growth Fund II after [designer and manufacturer Arcadia](https://www.afr.com/street-talk/private-equity-firm-whiteoak-backs-century-old-industrial-designer-20260315-p5oamt) and architecture modelling tool Avolution.

The board of Penfolds owner Treasury Wine Estates must be urgently overhauled to stop the company’s share price from sliding even further, says a former global chief operating officer of the company who went on to run Australia’s second-largest wine group, Accolade Wines.

Robert Foye was a key player in driving the aggressive growth of the Penfolds business in China before his abrupt exit in 2019, and said he was disheartened by the company’s decline, in which its shares have plunged to $3.73 from more than $8 a year ago.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.16%2C$multiply_4%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_44/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/d1c543c98987b86689f7a344172fc0ec1a55bd74)

Robert Foye says urgent action is needed to reverse Treasury’s slumping share price.

Foye, who owns a $1 million stake in Treasury, sent letters to chairman John Mullen saying that while he supported [chief executive Sam Fischer’s cost-cutting plan](https://www.afr.com/companies/retail/penfolds-owner-treasury-wine-axes-dividend-sets-deadline-for-surgery-20260109-p5nss7), the board needed to be urgently strengthened.

“Over the past two years, share owners have experienced a decline of more than 60 per cent in the stock price, driven by a series of strategic missteps, operational underperformance in the US and China, and what I believe to be an absence of deep operating expertise on the board,” Foye said.

Fischer, who started at Treasury as chief executive in October after running beer group Lion, plans to strip out costs and has set a mid-year deadline on deciding whether it will sell some of its high-profile brands.

Advertisement

Foye, who was [chief executive of Accolade Wines](https://www.afr.com/companies/agriculture/accolade-wines-ceo-robert-foye-quits-private-equity-backed-group-20240829-p5k6gv) – the owner of brands including Hardys, Grant Burge and Petaluma – from 2020 to 2024, before its name change to Vinarchy, said Treasury’s sliding value required immediate attention.

Texas-based Foye was global chief operating officer at Treasury for five years and was central in building up Penfolds sales in China before Beijing imposed punishing tariffs on Australian wine in 2020.

He said Treasury needed a “thorough review of the US business with the objective of doubling EBIT within five years, or pursuing strategic alternatives that maximise share owner value”.

Although the Penfolds brand has held up better than many others in an overall market where wine consumption is falling globally, cheaper wines have suffered as consumers drink less alcohol and shift away from wine.

Foye said Treasury should return to a geographic operating model “to eliminate the costly and inefficient dual-division structure”.

He said he had offered his services to the chairman as a non-executive board member.

Advertisement

Foye left Treasury in 2019 following a breach of the company’s internal code of conduct policies.

“In my view, the board requires a director with genuine operating expertise in the global wine and \[fast-moving consumer goods\] industry, as well as the US and China and Asia,” Foye said.

“Someone who has previously run these markets, managed these distributors, and built these teams. That experience does not currently exist at the board level.”

A TWE spokeswoman said “the board has no current plans to pursue the proposal referenced,” and it had the right mix of skills and experience.

MST analyst Craig Woolford said in February after the company suspended first-half dividends following a profit dive, that “Treasury Wines has a long journey to rebuild trust”.

Artificial intelligence infrastructure start-up Firmus is expected to be valued at nearly $7 billion after the world’s biggest company Nvidia doubled its investment in what’s slated to be its final capital raising as a private company.

Firmus was scrambling to finalise the third and final tranche of its whopper $1 billion-plus pre-IPO raising on Monday night, according to those close to the company who were not authorised to speak publicly. It includes a share sale to Nvidia and other strategic investors, and comes hours before its management make their maiden pitch to potential IPO investors in Asia on Tuesday.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.249%2C$multiply_3%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/25886d5bd64b22ab793405a554fcc9c999c02957)

Firmus co-chief executives Tim Rosenfield and Oliver Curtis are due to address potential Asia based investors on Tuesday.  Amrita Chandradas

Nvidia and the other strategic investors were expected to buy new Firmus shares at $144 each, a 15 per cent increase on its $US550 million November capital raising which valued the business at $6 billion, and four times the value of its $330 million round in September.

The latest round was only open to Firmus’ “strategic partners” including Nvidia and values the company at about $6.9 billion. The total raising size was understood to be smaller than the previous two.

It takes the company’s equity funds raised to more than $1 billion in the past year, and sees it turn its attention to becoming the largest IPO in Australia this decade in coming months.

Advertisement

The company declined to comment on the funding round, the roadshow or its public listing plans. It is expected to raise up to $3 billion in capital as part of the IPO and has Bank of America, JPMorgan, Morgans and Morgan Stanley introducing it to potential investors on a non-deal roadshow starting this week.

The seven-year-old company was founded by cousins Oliver Curtis and Tim Rosenfield and Curtis’ ex brother-in-law, Jonathan Levee, in Tasmania. It has attracted backers including Nvidia, Blackstone, and Temasek, an investment giant owned by the Singapore government.

It also counts former treasurer Joe Hockey, [ex-rugby league player Wes Maas](https://www.afr.com/property/residential/rich-lister-from-dubbo-takes-manly-trophy-home-spend-to-67m-20250212-p5lbop) and billionaire businessman James Packer as investors.

## Listing plans

Curtis was convicted of insider trading in 2016 and spent a year in prison following one of the most high-profile corporate criminal cases in Australia. He was recently [informally cleared to stay on as a director](https://www.afr.com/link/follow-20180101-p5zj6y) after the company goes public following high-level discussions with the ASX.

Final approval is not expected until Firmus’ prospectus is lodged and cleared by the ASX and the corporate regulator, the Australian Securities Investment Commission, but is seen as a formality according to three people close to the company.

Advertisement

Firmus wants to develop $73.3 billion worth of data centres alongside Australia’s CDC for what it calls “Project Southgate,” in partnership with Nvidia which is a chipmaker, customer and shareholder.

These facilities will be located in regional areas and fitted out with Firmus’ cooling technology, starting with an AI factory in Launceston, which is expected to open this year.

Its first AI factory is due to open in Melbourne in a matter of weeks after it signed a $600 million contract with an unnamed tech giant widely speculated to be Meta. Curtis has told investors he is seeking to sign a third large customer for a new AI factory before the IPO, which would materially increase Firmus’ near-term capital requirement.

Unlike traditional data centres which tend to use massive air conditioning units to keep their chips cool, Firmus uses liquid immersion cooling and direct to chip cooling which the company says uses significantly less energy and barely any water.

Nvidia, which manufactures advanced chips known as general processing units needed for AI processing and to train the so-called large language models that underpin platforms like ChatGPT, has a market capitalisation of $US4.3 trillion ($6.2 trillion), making it the largest company in the world.

Nvidia has invested billions of dollars into partnerships with data centre players like Firmus over the past two years. It’s considered to be the central player in the AI economy, often providing the chips, but also investing in the data centre businesses and becoming a customer through its subsidiary DGX Cloud. Last year, for instance, it agreed to invest $US100 billion into OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT.

But Firmus is the only company in Asia which considers Nvidia to be a supplier, investor and customer. Its partnership is core to why Firmus has been able to raise so much money and sign other tech giants on as customers.
